step_by_step_thinking:
Your answer:

We are asked to identify what biofilms formed on tooth surfaces are called. 

Biofilms on teeth consist of bacteria, food debris, saliva proteins, etc., forming a sticky film that adheres to enamel — this is known clinically as dental plaque. While enamel (option A) is hard tissue and not a biofilm; dental caries (option B) is decay caused by acid from plaque over time; and saliva (option D) contributes to oral health but isn't the biofilm itself.
raw_response:
Your answer:

We are asked to identify what biofilms formed on tooth surfaces are called. 

Biofilms on teeth consist of bacteria, food debris, saliva proteins, etc., forming a sticky film that adheres to enamel — this is known clinically as dental plaque. While enamel (option A) is hard tissue and not a biofilm; dental caries (option B) is decay caused by acid from plaque over time; and saliva (option D) contributes to oral health but isn't the biofilm itself.

Answer: C: Dental plaque.
